name: Silas Weir
whyHere: You finished something. Completely. It nearly got you exiled, and you are still not sure they were wrong.

inventory.name: Traveler's Pack
inventory.description: A worn canvas pack with good straps — bedroll, flint, mess kit, fifty small comforts of the road.

inventory.name: Waterskin
inventory.description: Boiled and refilled at the last clean spring. Worth more every week the river runs gray.

inventory.name: Belt Knife
inventory.description: A plain working blade — more tool than weapon, but it has been both.

inventory.name: Hemp Rope (30 ft)
inventory.description: Tarred hemp, knotted every arm's length. The difference between a fall and a climb.

inventory.name: Preserved Trail Parcel
inventory.description: Aldric's smokehouse goods — dried river perch and salted pork, cloth-wrapped and knotted. Holds a week on the road. Two parcels.

inventory.name: Rune-Craft Kit
inventory.description: A stub of hard cooper's wax, a half-spent vial of lamp-black in pine pitch, and a strip of tooling-grade oiled leather — for pressing, staining, and binding marks into worked surfaces.

inventory.name: Assembled Mid-Weight Spear
inventory.description: A seasoned ash shaft six feet long with the mid-weight iron tip road-lashed at the socket throat — wide-cut blade, cross-guard shoulder, oiled leather binding. Not finish work, but it won't shift on a boar.

inventory.name: Boar Tusks (pair)
inventory.description: Ivory-yellow and dense at the base — a matched pair from the scrub oak ridge bull. Raw material for something, not yet decided.

inventory.name: Valve Failure Timeline (note)
inventory.description: A small torn page in Mara's hand — four sleeve blank sales, four failure dates, two customer names. World-day minus-eighteen: Fen's intake. World-day minus-nine: east furlong farmer.

inventory.name: Contact Names Scrap (Sorel, Leth)
inventory.description: A chalk-written scrap from Mara — two names from the old pass circuit with Edge District ties. Sorel: spice-broker, wintered in the Edge District two years. Leth: archivist type, likely still there.

inventory.name: Hard Tallow Block
inventory.description: Rendered and pressed flat — good for leather, boots, rope-ends, and a dozen trail needs.

inventory.name: Oilcloth Fold
inventory.description: Heavy enough to shed a night of rain, light enough to pack small.

inventory.name: Pine-Tar Salve Tin
inventory.description: Dara's recipe — for cracked skin, rope-burn, and anything the cold gets into.

inventory.name: River Whetstone (Pell's)
inventory.description: A smooth river-flat whetstone — Pell's own, returned to the bench twice and given away once. Now in her pocket, not yours.

inventory.name: Fendt's Testimony (folded page)
inventory.description: A single page in Fendt's hand — dates, seventh-band designations, the nine-day gap, Grael named as the receiving authority, and the preliminary concern notice receipt date. Now sealed inside the document roll alongside Silas's chain-record, bound with brass wire and Calvar's wax. One copy. The other
inventory.provenance: Written by Fendt; carried by Silas from the Edge District; now sealed with a second hand into a complete record.

inventory.name: Brass Wire Coil (fine)
inventory.description: A coil of fine brass wire from Fendt's shop — used for sealing sample vessels; precise enough for binding or rune-work.

inventory.name: Chalk Dust Tin (wax-sealed)
inventory.description: Brilliant white chalk dust in a sealed tin — for marking surfaces in poor light or wet conditions.

inventory.name: Pre-Transition Filter Cloth
inventory.description: A tight-woven square of filtering cloth, pre-Transition manufacture — can strain contaminated water through multiple passes.

inventory.name: Pilfered Camp Tin
inventory.description: A small tin from the opportunists' pack — likely taken from someone's unattended camp. Contents unknown.

inventory.name: Trade-Knives (pair)
inventory.description: Two small belt-trade knives confiscated from the road opportunists — plain, serviceable, nothing special.

deeds.description: Raised a cleaned rabbit from the dead in the upper meadow — no ritual, no preparation, hands and will and the valley's old grain answering
Struck a fair provisioning arrangement with Aldric the smokehouse man — hunting for the village in exchange for food and supplies, and offered to keep watch on what's damaging the river catch.
Silas Weir bound death-runes into the assembled spear at an open-sky fire, with Pell Marsh's maker's mark seated into the blade and Huginn witness at the stone ring.
Coordinated a clean boar kill in the north wood — Corvin landed the strike, Silas held the line and read the ending with a bound spear
Brought down the bull boar on the scrub oak ridge with Pell — a clean hunt despite a misfire in the rune-work.
Delivered a dressed bull and sow to Aldric's smokehouse — a significant haul by village standards, carried down from the north wood by three hunters.
Named the repaired death-bound spear Memory in Pell's forge at dawn — a word that held.
Kissed Pell Marsh at the forge bench at dawn — after a full night repairing Memory together and naming it over old wine.
Persuaded Fendt to commit his testimony to paper and walk to Millbrook — moved the falsified ledger thread from evidence-held to evidence-carried.
Persuaded Calvar — pre-Transition filtration engineer and author of the seventh-band specification — to leave the Edge District and travel to Millbrook to attest against Overseer Grael
Neutralized a relay figure in the cut-lane without violence or alarm — The Ended Threat, Wildcraft, and The Shadow Work braided into a clean exit for Calvar and the document.
Showed mercy to road opportunists after disarming them — named the broad man's grief, gave them provisions, and sent them to honest work rather than leaving them in the mud
Called the key players of the water crisis to Pell's forge for an evening council — Dara, Aldric, Fendt, Mara, and the engineer Calvar, all in one room.
Organized the valley's water-crisis response into four parallel working threads — engineering, logistics, supply, and accounts — with named leads for each, in front of every stakeholder

turns.summary: The pass road deposits you onto the Plateau's rim like a seam giving way — one step, stone underfoot
turns.narration:
The pass road deposits you onto the Plateau's rim like a seam giving way — one step, stone underfoot, the valley's breath behind you, and then the Edge District opens in front of you in the early-spring dawn with the whole morning's light already working inside it. The photonic-crystal towers catch the low angle and braid it into something deliberate: beams of amber and cold white running along the street-guide channels in the paving stones, pooling at intersections, climbing the tower faces in slow coronal sweeps that have nothing accidental about them. Someone chose every angle. Someone is always choosing. Huginn lands on your pack-frame without ceremony and says nothing, which means what the district holds does not have an imminent ending — only the dense, layered quality of a place where several endings have been arranged and are being waited upon.

The Edge District in dawn-light is not the same as a thing honestly illuminated. It is the same principle as the valley below — clarity and revelation — but here clarity is a craft, and what you see on a given street corner is precisely the version of the street corner someone decided you should see. The towers are refracting now, and the light on the paving stones shifts by a quarter-step as you walk the outer track toward the district's low buildings: a man grinding a lens at an open-air bench does not look up, but his work-mirror catches you and holds your image longer than physics requires. The apprentices running beam-duel drills on the mid-plaza are genuinely harmless, but they are also watching the threshold where the pass road meets district stone, and they broke off a sequence when you crossed it.

The outer settlement from the overhang looked like gray stone and two or three chimney-smokes. It is that, and it is also a receiving surface — this is where the Plateau takes its first reading of what the valley sends up. Mara's two names are in your pocket in chalk-written scrap: Sorel, spice-broker, wintered here, possibly still present. Leth, archivist, Sorel expects her resident. The district's accountability board is visible from where you stand, bolted to the south face of the nearest tower — and even from here, in the first amber of the morning, you can see a name written in red chalk on it, the surrounding marks in pale gray, that one name staining the board with a color someone chose for denunciation. Fendt. The chalk is recent enough that a corner has smeared in the night damp but not washed out. Beside it, the space where an accompanying record should have been filed is conspicuously, unnaturally bare.

Huginn tilts its head at the board from your shoulder, a single slow rotation, and then looks away — toward the district's interior, toward a particular chimney beginning to breathe, toward the part of the morning that has not yet shown you what it means to.

turns.player: Ask Pell if she thinks we should check out the building Huginn was eyeing first, or something else.
turns.summary: Pell advises starting with Huginn's building before pursuing Leth — concrete lead before cold knocking.
turns.narration:
You turn to Pell, tilting your head toward the building Huginn has been watching — the one with the narrow colonnade and the pale stone face, second from the corner, the one that has exactly the same anonymous frontage as every other administrative building on this block except for the fact that your bird will not look away from it. "Huginn's been fixed on that one since we arrived," you say. "Worth starting there, or do you want to find Leth first?"

Pell looks at the building, then at Huginn, then back at you with the flat considering expression she uses when she's pricing a job. "Your bird finds ends," she says. "If it's watching that building, something in there is concluding." A pause. "Leth's a name on a scrap. She might be anywhere in this district, and we don't know her face." She shifts the strap of her pack, glances down the light-guide street toward the district's interior where the morning's amber beams are already drawing early workers out of doorways. "The building is here and it's not moving. I'd start there — see what Huginn's watching before we go knocking on archive doors asking questions that make people close them."

Huginn shifts its weight on your pack-frame and says nothing. From Huginn, that reads as agreement, or at least as patience running very thin.

turns.summary: Pell advises starting at Huginn's building; the party faces the recessed door on the light-guide street.
turns.narration:
Pell finishes her thought without fanfare: "Cold knock at Leth's without a face or an address wastes the morning. Your bird has a face. Start there." She adjusts her pack-strap once, settles it, and waits — not impatiently, but with the particular quality of stillness she uses when the next move is yours to make.

The building sits a half-dozen paces ahead, pale stone face catching the amber from the light-guide channel running along the street's left edge. It is narrow for its height — a colonnade of four thin pillars framing a recessed door, the kind of frontage that is designed to be overlooked. No signage. One small window at the second floor, shuttered. The door is a plain dark wood panel, slightly lighter at the handle-side edge: used regularly, and recently.

Huginn has not moved. It sits on your pack-frame with the unhurried fixity of something that already knows how this ends — head angled perhaps fifteen degrees off true, one eye on the door, the other on the second-floor shutter. Not alarm. Not urgency. The patient attention of a thing that showed up because something here is concluding, and intends to see it through.

The district is waking around you. Farther down the street a pair of workers move through a doorway, amber-lit from below. A light-guide intersection pulses once — a slow cold-white sweep — and returns to amber. Nobody on this block is paying you particular attention yet.

turns.player: Ask Huginn plainly — what is ending in there, and how long does it have
turns.summary: Huginn confirms: someone inside is concluding something — hours, not days. Pell says they don't wait.
turns.narration:
Huginn turns one eye toward you when you ask — not the whole head, just the near eye, the way it does when the answer is already obvious to it and it is being patient about the gap.

A croak. Low, deliberate. Then a pause timed like punctuation.

It looks back at the building. Looks at the shuttered window. Looks at the door, where the light at the handle-edge is a thin hot line in the morning cool.

You read it the way you've learned to read it: someone inside, ending near, not violent — a departure, a decision already made, a thing being given away or surrendered. The shape of it is close: hours, not days. The window, not the door, is where Huginn's attention keeps returning.

Pell watches the exchange without comment, then says, quietly: "How long?"

"Hours," you tell her.

She nods once, the kind of nod that files a fact without editorializing. "Then we don't wait."

turns.player: Ask Pell to take the door while you circle to the building's side or rear — see if there's another way in or out before whoever is inside finishes and leaves
turns.summary: Silas circles the building; Pell holds the front door; a rear service exit is found — not latched, a bundle left beside it.
turns.narration:
You catch Pell's eye and tip your chin toward the building's far edge. 'Take the door,' you say quietly. 'Give me a minute to check the flanks — I want to know if there's a side exit before whoever's in there walks out the front.' She reads it without needing it repeated. One short nod, pack-strap adjusted, and she moves to a point three paces left of the recessed door — not blocking it, not announcing herself, just occupying the space that makes a person choose to go through the front.

You go right, stepping off the light-guide channel into the narrow stone gap between this building and its neighbor. The alley is barely a shoulder's width — cold still, in morning shadow, the photonic amber from the street not reaching here. You move slowly, one hand trailing the pale stone, eyes adjusting. The building's flank is plain: no windows at ground level, a drainage channel cut into the base of the wall, and higher up on the second floor, the back of the same shuttered window Huginn has been watching. Shutters, closed, no light leaking from the back edge — whatever is lit in that room faces the street.

At the rear corner, the building meets a service lane running parallel to the main street — wider than the gap you came through, enough for a handcart. There is a door: small, lower than the front, iron-banded, and it is not latched. A thin dark stripe shows between door-edge and frame, the same quality of not-quite-closed that a person in a hurry, or a person who does not intend to stay, would leave behind them. A bundle leans against the exterior wall beside it — cloth-wrapped, tied with a single cord, the size of a person's working kit or a season's worth of records.

Huginn, still on your pack-frame, shifts its weight. It does not look at the bundle. It looks at the door.

turns.player: Look through the door into the room beyond. If it's empty, find a shadow to shadow step into without opening the door and remain perfectly still in the shadow to observer from inside.
turns.summary: Silas shadowsteps into the rear storage room and holds position — one person upstairs, packing, almost done.
turns.narration:
The rear door stands barely a finger's width from its frame — the latch tongue not quite seated, a thin dark stripe of interior warmth at the edge. You press your eye to the gap.

A storage room. Low ceiling, shelves mostly bare, one oil lamp burning on a hook to the left — the light catching dust motes that have not settled yet, meaning someone moved through here recently. Beyond the storage room, an open inner doorway leads to a staircase, and you can hear it: quiet, deliberate footsteps on the upper floor, back and forth, the weight-pattern of packing. One person. The bundle by the door outside was placed there already, which means they are almost done. At the far right edge of the storage room, a wedge of shadow falls from the shelf-unit — deep enough, real enough.

You ease back from the gap, breathe once, and find the shadow at your feet — the alley's cold dark against the building's base — and the one inside the room, connected by the dark line of the door's crack. Shadowstep is a step between two places that are already the same place in the dark. You take it. The world folds without sound.

You are inside. The oil lamp is four feet to your left, throwing its circle away from you. The shelf-shadow holds you completely. The footsteps continue upstairs — unhurried, no break in rhythm, no sign they felt anything. You press your back to the shelf and listen. The room smells of old paper, lamp-oil, and something sharper underneath — a chemical residue you recognize as a water-treatment solvent, the kind used in filtration array work. The inner doorway frames the bottom of the stairs. Through it, the cadence of someone moving between surfaces: a desk, a shelf, a case being latched. One person packing the last of what they intend to take.

turns.player: Shadowstep silently to the top of the stairs
turns.summary: Silas shadowsteps silently to the landing — the figure at the desk, case latched, last paper folded, does not know he is there.
turns.narration:
The shadow at the base of the staircase and the shadow at the top are one thing — you feel that before you move, the way you feel the cold before you touch iron. The lamp behind you throws its light toward the front of the room and away from the stairs entirely, leaving a column of genuine dark from floor-sill to landing-edge. Both ends shadowed. Clean.

You step.

There is no sound. Not the soft landing of careful feet, not the held breath of someone trying to be quiet — nothing. The Stepping Dark earns its name in moments like this one: you are simply at the top of the stairs, the way a thought arrives, and the stair itself never knew you.

The upper floor opens before you. A single room, low-beamed, lit by one narrow shutter cracked two fingers wide — the morning light coming through at an angle, hitting the opposite wall as a pale bar. A figure stands at a desk with their back to the stairhead: compact, dressed in the muted gray-brown of someone who does not want to be a bright thing in a bright place. A traveling case sits latched and buckled on the floor beside their boots. They are folding the last paper — something flat, official-looking, tucked into a coat's inner pocket — and their hands are unhurried in the way hands are when a decision has already been made and there is nothing left to feel about it.

They have not heard you. The room smells of the same water-treatment solvent that the storage room below held, sharper up here, and something else beneath it — old ink, dried carefully, the smell of records that lived in the same place for years and are now about to leave it.

turns.player: use both the attended end and order sense to get the full picture.
turns.summary: Silas reads the room through combined craft — the figure is departing on his own schedule, not fleeing; the role is ending, the man is not.
turns.narration:
You don't move. You stand at the landing-head and let the two crafts settle into each other — unhurried, the way you'd press two edges together to check the fit before committing the join.

The Attended End touches the room first. It goes to what it always goes to: the nearest ending. And the ending here is not the figure. The ending is the role — the position, the function, the thread of work this person has been part of. That thread has three, maybe four days left, and it is unraveling from the outside in, not the inside out. Whoever ends it won't be the figure pocketing that paper. Something else is closing the account.

Order-sense follows, quieter and wider. You read the design the room is part of — not the room itself, but the structure it serves. The desk was placed to face the door. The shelves were built for a working archive. The lamp hook was positioned for someone spending long hours here. This was a functional post, not a residence, and the person at the desk has been its occupant long enough that the room carries the shape of their habits. The paper now in the coat pocket is the last record of a finished task — the kind of document you file, not the kind you take, unless you don't expect to be asked for it again. The traveling case says the same thing. This isn't a man between assignments. This is a man who has decided he is done being found here.

The two readings fit together like matched valve faces: the ending is institutional and closing fast, the departure is voluntary and already decided, and the bundle by the rear door was placed there this morning, not last night. He is not fleeing. He is departing on a schedule he set himself — which means he believes he has time, and which means the window Huginn named is exactly correct. Hours.

turns.player: Say simply "Fendt", not loudly, not softly.  He will likely startle, but don't make any overtly threatening moves, yet.
turns.summary: Silas names Fendt quietly at the landing-head; the man startles, reads him, and stills — assessment, not flight.
turns.narration:
You say the name the way you'd set a tool down on a bench — no weight added, no weight withheld. "Fendt."

He startles. The traveling case scrapes against his boot as his hand snaps to it; his whole body turns in one compressed motion, the instinct of a man who has been waiting for this, who has perhaps been practicing not waiting for it. He is mid-forties, hollow at the cheeks, wearing the kind of coat that was pressed last week and has been slept in since. His eyes find you at the landing-head and hold there — not bolting, not reaching for anything, just reading you with the particular attention of someone who has been calculating odds for a long time and wants to know which column you fall in.

Huginn, perched at your shoulder, says nothing. Its eye is on the man at the case. The silence it keeps is a comment.

Fendt's breathing slows deliberately — you can see him make himself do it. His hand is still on the case handle but his weight has shifted back from flight into something else: assessment. He takes in the landing, the shadow you came from, the spear across your back. He does not look at the door behind him. That is information too. "You came through the back," he says, and it is not a question, and there is something in his voice that is almost — not relief, but its structural cousin. Someone found him before the board did.

turns.player: Use Order-Sense on Fendt himself — read the design he's part of, what role he played in the filtration system, and what piece of it only he still holds.
turns.summary: Order-Sense fails in the thin substrate — Silas is left with Fendt's silence and the unread paper in his coat.
turns.narration:
The lattice here is thin — you knew that before you tried. You let Order-Sense settle into Fendt the way you'd press a fingertip to a surface to test for heat, and it simply... slides off. Not blocked, not refused. Insufficient. The substrate can't sustain the reach. What you get is the surface: a man with a case, a coat, a paper in the inner pocket, and the particular stillness of someone who has decided to stop moving until he knows what you are.

The craft gives you nothing about the design he belongs to. It can't thread the full picture together in this district's thin light.

Which means you're left with what you came in with: his reaction to his own name spoken by a stranger who emerged from a shadow. He has not run. He has not threatened. He has *assessed* — and he is still assessing, which means he has not yet decided you are a problem. That is something. Not much, but something.

Huginn shifts on your shoulder and says nothing. The room holds the morning light coming through the shutter's cracked two fingers, and Fendt's hand rests on the case, and the silence between you has the particular weight of a man waiting to learn whether the next sentence will cost him.

turns.player: Tell him you're not from the council — and that you don't care about the board, you care about the river.
turns.summary: Silas names the river; Fendt stills and opens the question back.
turns.narration:
Fendt's assessment doesn't soften exactly, but something in it shifts — a reweighting, a column being moved.

You keep your position at the landing-head. No step toward him, no hand raised. "I'm not from the council," you say, and you let the flatness of it carry its own truth. "The board doesn't interest me. The river does."

A beat. Huginn makes no sound on your shoulder, which in its way is a comment.

Fendt's hand stays on the case but his grip changes — less the grip of a man about to run, more the grip of a man who has just heard something that doesn't fit the shape of what he was braced for. He looks at you the way someone looks at a locked door they've just been told they can walk through. Calculating whether it's a trap. Deciding how much it matters if it is.

"The river," he says. Not a question. Testing the shape of it in his mouth, like a word in a language he used to know.

He doesn't sit down. He doesn't offer you a seat either. But he doesn't pick up the case, and he doesn't take another step toward the staircase. His eyes go to Huginn once — briefly, the way people do — then come back to you. "Three weeks," he says, quieter now, something technical bleeding into his voice despite himself. "I have been watching numbers that didn't add up for three weeks before anyone put my name on that board." A pause. "What do you know about the river?"

turns.player: Use Deathsense on the paper in his coat — not on Fendt, on what that folded record is ending. Read what its disappearance closes off.
turns.summary: Deathsense reads the paper in Fendt's coat — the real filtration readings exist, and their suppression is what's ending.
turns.narration:
Silas doesn't look at Fendt's face. He looks at the coat.

Deathsense settles into the paper the way water finds a low point — without effort, without announcement. The record is ending. Not the paper itself; the paper will survive the journey in Fendt's pocket, will probably be burned or buried somewhere down whatever road he's chosen. What's ending is *what it could have said to someone official*. Its moment of being evidence. Three weeks of logged readings, real ones, the numbers Fendt actually measured before someone else's figures replaced them in the formal ledger — and that function, the function of being a corrective record, is closing off. He took it rather than filed it, and the taking is what ended it.

You learn two things from the shape of that ending. First: the paper exists, which means the falsified readings in the formal ledger have a counterpart. Second: whoever replaced those readings with false ones did it *after* Fendt had already committed the real figures somewhere. He didn't falsify and then keep a copy of the truth out of guilt. He kept the truth and then watched someone else falsify in his name and over his initials, and the paper in his coat is the only version of the event that says so.

The deathsense doesn't tell you who did the replacing. It tells you what closing this record off costs. There will be nothing left in this district to say what the river was actually reading, or when it changed, or how fast.

Fendt is still watching you. He hasn't moved.

turns.player: Call Pell up, then tell him to tell you both everything.
turns.summary: Silas signals Pell up; both face Fendt and ask for the full account from three weeks ago.
turns.narration:
The decision arrives clean and simple: Fendt needs to say it once, to both of them.

You step back to the stairhead and knock the wall twice — your knuckles on the plaster, not loud, the kind of sound that travels a specific distance and no further. You keep your eyes on Fendt while you wait. Huginn shifts weight on your shoulder, a single adjustment, then goes still again. Fendt watches the door at the bottom of the stairs with the expression of a man who has just had the number of people in the room double without his consent, and is running the arithmetic on whether that changes anything.

It takes Pell under two minutes. You hear the rear service door, the soft transit of boots on the storage floor, the staircase taking her weight without complaint. She comes up with the careful economy of someone who entered a building she wasn't sure about and is confirming it as she goes — hand not quite on the tool at her belt, eyes moving to Fendt before they find you. She reads the room: a desk, a case, a man who has been somewhere between leaving and staying since before she arrived. She doesn't say anything. She takes a position two steps from the stair-head, angled so neither the door nor Fendt requires her to turn, and waits.

Fendt looks at her, then back at you. 'Two of you,' he says. It is not a protest — it is a man updating his working model of the situation.

'Tell us both,' you say. 'Everything you watched happen. Start with three weeks ago, when the numbers stopped matching.'

The hand on the case doesn't move. But after a moment, Fendt lets out a breath through his nose — long, deliberate, the kind of exhale that closes one account before opening another — and begins.

turns.player: Let him talk without interruption — listen and watch for the places where the story costs him something.
turns.summary: Fendt gives his full account — seventh-band particulate, Overseer Grael's name, and the falsified ledger he caught too late to stop.
turns.narration:
Fendt talks.

He starts with the numbers — the way engineers always start, with what they can prove. Three weeks ago the intake readings began showing elevated particulate in the seventh band: not dramatically, not enough to trigger a mandatory report, but enough that a careful man who had been watching that band for eleven years noticed it wasn't noise. He logged it in the formal ledger. He logged it the next day, and the day after. He adjusted the filtration array calibration twice — standard response — and the numbers didn't move the way they should have. The particulate was consistent in a way that natural variation isn't. Consistent and directional: shifting slowly upstream, which is not how sediment works.

That's where his voice flattens and he looks at a point on the cleared desk rather than at either of you. He filed a preliminary concern notice to his supervisor — Overseer Grael, he says the name the way you say the name of a weather system you've been living under — and three days later he came in to find the formal ledger had been updated under his own initials. New figures. Seven readings, rewritten. The particulate was gone from the record. He stood in the array office with the revised ledger in his hands and looked at it for a long time, and then he went home and wrote his own figures down from memory on a single sheet of paper and put it in his coat.

That's where it costs him something: not when he names Grael, not when he describes the falsification, but when he says 'from memory' — you catch it, the small tightening at the corner of his mouth, the word that admits he was not sure his memory was good enough, that he spent that night comparing what he had written against what he thought he knew, checking himself, and that he is still not entirely certain the paper in his pocket is exact. He is a man who trusts his instruments and was given cause to doubt his instruments. What it did to him is still visible if you know where to look.

He stops. He looks at Pell, then at you. Huginn looks back at him without blinking. 'The seventh-band readings are what they are,' he says finally. 'If someone wanted to hide where the contamination is coming from upstream — really hide it, not just delay it — those are the figures you'd need gone.' Pell has not moved and has not spoken. She's filing.

turns.player: Let Pell ask him questions next.
turns.summary: Pell takes over the questioning — Fendt confirms Grael was named by band designation in the original concern notice, meaning the suppression was precise and deliberate.
turns.narration:
Silas steps back from the desk — not far, just enough to make the geometry plain. He rests his weight on his back foot and goes quiet, the spear's heel dropping to the floor beside him with a soft knock. A clear signal to Pell, if she wants it.

She takes it. No preamble. She moves to the position Silas vacated and looks at Fendt the way she looks at a joint that might be failing — straight on, without hurry.

"The seven readings that were removed," she says. "Were they consecutive, or spread across the three weeks?"

Fendt blinks, recalibrates. He had been reading Silas as the one who would ask questions; now the frame has shifted and he is adjusting. His hand drops fully from the traveling case. "Spread," he says, after a moment. "Not evenly. Four in the first two weeks, three in the last. The gap between the fifth and sixth was nine days — I noticed it at the time but I didn't know what I was noticing."

Pell nods, exactly once, the way she nods when a measurement confirms something she already suspected. "And your preliminary concern notice to Grael — did you name the seventh band specifically, or just report elevated particulate?"

Fendt's jaw tightens. "I named it. By band designation. I was precise."

Pell doesn't say anything for a moment. She glances at Silas — not for instruction, just sharing the weight of what that means. Grael knew exactly what he was suppressing, and he knew it from Fendt's own language.

Huginn tilts his head toward the shuttered window, unhurried.

turns.player: Name Leth to Fendt — the archivist who noticed the archive breach. See if the name means anything to him and whether the two threads connect.
turns.summary: Leth's name lands — Fendt knows her, knows the archive complaint, and the two threads are now one in the room.
turns.narration:
The name lands quietly.

"Leth," you say. Not a question — just the name, offered into the space between you and Fendt the way you'd set a tool on a workbench to see what the other person does with it. "Archivist. Edge District. She wrote to a contact of ours last month about someone removing material from the archive room. Specific material. Someone who knew exactly what they were looking for."

Fendt goes very still.

It is not the stillness of a man who doesn't recognize the name. It is the stillness of a man who has just watched two separate problems reveal themselves as one problem, and is standing at the join, and does not yet know whether that makes his situation better or worse.

"Leth filed a complaint," he says, finally. His voice has gone careful again — the deliberate engineer's cadence, the one he uses when he is testing whether what he says will be used against him. "Two weeks ago. To the district records office. Someone had gone through the historical calibration files — the old array-setup records, pre-Transition provenance documentation, intake charts from before my time. She flagged it as a breach because the removal was too precise to be accidental." He pauses. "The records office filed her complaint in the inactive stack. I saw the copy because Leth — because she is the kind of person who makes sure someone else has a copy." A brief, grim recognition crosses his face. "The material that was removed. Did your contact say what specifically?"

Pell glances at you once — the same sidelong read she uses when a joint has just confirmed what she suspected and now she wants to know if you caught it too.

Huginn shifts on your shoulder. One feather, settling.

turns.player: Share what Sorel passed on — the breach was targeted, the actor moved fast, left nothing except the absence of what they took, and did it before the pass was open. See what Fendt makes of the sequence.
turns.summary: Silas lays out the pre-pass sequence; Fendt names it: not a cover-up, a preparation.
turns.narration:
The room is quiet enough that you can hear the shutter-light shifting as the sun moves.

You lay it out plainly — what Sorel passed on, what Leth wrote back, the sequence as you have it: someone entered the archive room before the pass opened. Not after. Before. They moved fast and clean, left no sign of method, and took only the historical calibration files — old array-setup records, pre-Transition provenance documentation, intake charts from before Fendt's time. Not recent figures. Not anything that would implicate anyone living. The oldest layer, the foundational layer — the material that would tell you where the system came from and what it was originally designed to do.

Then they were gone. The pass opened. Three weeks later the seventh-band readings started trending upstream.

Fendt listens without moving. His hand is still off the traveling case. When you finish he exhales — not relief, not distress, something older than either. A man who has been holding a partial picture for weeks and has just been handed the frame. "The calibration files," he says, placing the words carefully. "I told myself it was archival theft — provenance records for resale, or array-setup data to replicate elsewhere. But if the sequence starts with knowledge—" He stops. Looks at the window. Back. "Someone who knew the seventh band mattered before the readings started. Before I filed anything." Quieter, almost to himself: "They weren't covering a mistake. They were clearing the ground ahead of time."

Pell says nothing from the desk. But her eyes move to you. Huginn shifts one foot on your shoulder — the unhurried way he moves when something has been confirmed rather than discovered.

turns.player: Give Fendt the floor — he has been calculating since the frame closed; let him ask what he reaches for when the picture is finally complete.
turns.summary: Fendt asks the direct question — who sent you, and where does his real paper go.
turns.narration:
The silence after the last piece falls into place is a different kind of silence than the one that was here before. The earlier quiet was Fendt's — managed, careful, a man deciding what to name. This one is fuller. The frame is complete and he is standing inside it.

You open your hand toward him. Not a gesture of invitation so much as a removal of pressure — the questioning is done, the picture is done, and whatever comes next is his to begin. Pell stays where she is at the desk but she has gone still in the way she goes still when she is listening rather than assessing. Huginn shifts one foot on your shoulder and says nothing. The shutter-light has moved a finger's width up the wall since Sorel's name was first spoken.

Fendt is quiet for a moment. Then he does something you have not seen him do yet: he sits down. Not in a chair — the room has none — but on the edge of the cleared desk, slowly, like a man whose legs have finally registered that they have been holding a long weight. He looks at his hands.

"The paper in my coat," he says. "My real readings. I was going to take it to the district records office this morning and file it there, because the array-office copy is gone and I needed it somewhere it couldn't follow me." He pauses. "I don't think that works anymore. If the records office already filed Leth's complaint in the inactive stack—" He stops. Lets that die on its own. "So what I reach for," he says, "is you. Which is not a comfortable thing to reach for, because I don't know what you are or who sent you, and I've been in this building since before first light trying to work out which column you fall in." He looks up. Not at Pell — at you. "The paper goes somewhere it survives. That's the only thing I actually need. Everything else — the board, Grael, the rest of it — that's already moving whether I do anything or not." He exhales slowly. "So. Who sent you, and where does the paper go?"

turns.player: First, ask him what the missing record would have said.
turns.summary: Fendt names the erasure: the calibration files proved the seventh-band blind spot was a design specification, not an accident — their removal was the erasure of authorship.
turns.narration:
Fendt's hands settle on his knees. The traveling case at his feet doesn't move.

"The calibration files they took," he says, "were the original array-setup records. The intake baselines. What the filtration system was built to read, and what it was built to *ignore*." He says the last word the way you say a name you've been avoiding. "Every filtration array has a tolerance band — readings it flags, readings it files past. The seventh-band particulate sits outside most arrays' active monitoring window. You'd only know to look for it if you knew it was there to look for." He pauses. "Those files would have shown that someone, at setup, *built in* that blind spot. Not an oversight. A specification. Written in at the foundation layer and carried forward every time the array was recalibrated."

He looks at Pell, then back at you.

"What they took was the proof that the seventh band was always supposed to be invisible. My readings broke through the tolerance margin by accident — eleven years of accumulation. The particulate crossed the threshold I was actually monitoring." A flat sound, not quite a laugh. "If the original specs had still been in that room, a records office could have traced the design decision back to whoever authored it. Named them. Now they can't. The absence is the erasure." He touches his coat pocket — not reaching for the paper, just confirming it's there. "What's in here is three weeks of what the seventh band actually looks like when you aren't designed to miss it. It doesn't name who built the blind spot. But it proves the blind spot exists."

Huginn makes no sound. The shutter-light has moved another finger's width.

turns.player: Ask what the original array-setup records would have named — who authored the filtration specification, and how far back that design decision goes. If Fendt knows the provenance chain, he may know whose work this is even without the file.
turns.summary: Fendt names the design as deliberate and old — a system built to watch the seventh band, with documentation structured so erasing the authorship required removing only one layer.
turns.narration:
The shutter-light has moved another finger's width. Fendt's hands are still on his knees.

"Who authored it," you say. Not a question exactly — more a direction, naming the place the conversation has been walking toward since he said *specification*. "The original filtration array. The setup records that are now gone. Even without the file — you've worked this system for eleven years. Do you know whose design it was?"

Fendt looks at his hands for a moment. Not evasion; a man sorting what he knows from what he inferred, doing it honestly.

"The array predates me by fifteen years," he says. "Pre-Transition provenance. The setup documentation had a supervisor's mark — I saw it twice, when I was first brought on for orientation, and once when I pulled the baseline files to check a calibration drift seven years ago. I didn't study it. You don't study the founding documents of a system you're being trained to maintain." A breath. "The mark was a glyph, not a name. Council notation — the kind that traces to a department, not a person. But the intake charts had a second mark in the margin. Smaller. The kind engineers put there for themselves." He touches his coat pocket without quite meaning to. "I can't read the glyph. I wrote it down once, years ago, in a notebook I don't have anymore. But I remember the shape of the hand. Whoever signed those margins had a very particular way of forming their threes."

Pell's chin lifts a fraction. She's filed something.

"The calibration files would have named the department," Fendt continues, "and the department's records would have named the engineer. That's the chain. Without the files, you have a shape. A method. The CCW stain pattern on the valve housings, the way the seventh band sits outside every active monitoring window in this district—" He stops. "Someone built a system that could watch a specific particulate band, and then built the documentation so that if you wanted to erase the watching, you only had to remove one layer. The foundational layer. Whoever took those files knew the chain before they walked into that archive room."

The room holds that.

"I cannot give you a name," Fendt says. "But I can tell you the design is old. Older than me. And it was built to be used."

turns.player: Step back and let Pell name whatever she filed when Fendt mentioned the hand that forms threes.
turns.summary: Pell names what she filed — her father was a filtration engineer who left the Plateau after the Transition, and she would know his distinctive hand.
turns.narration:
You step back another half-pace and look at Pell — the small shift in your weight is enough. She's been holding something since Fendt said the phrase: *a distinctive hand forming threes*. You give her the room.

She doesn't rush it. Her eyes move from the traveling case to Fendt's coat pocket to his face, taking the triangle slow. Then: "My father apprenticed at the Radiant council archive for three years before he came down to the valley. He had a way with threes — the loop closing before the downstroke, the numeral leaning left. He called it an old surveyor's habit. Said it came from reading gradients." She pauses, not for effect. "He was a filtration engineer before he was a blacksmith. He left the Plateau the season after the Great Transition."

The room does not shift. The shutter-light is exactly where it was. But something in the geometry of the three of you has changed — Fendt's hands have gone still on his knees in a new way, not the stillness of a man waiting but of a man who has just found the shape of something he'd given up finding. Huginn makes no sound at all. You think about the margin notation, the lost notebook, the glyph Fendt wrote down once and cannot place. You think about a blacksmith's daughter who looks at torque signatures and names them without being shown twice.

Fendt says, carefully: "You would know the hand."

"I would know the hand," Pell says. "Show me the original, and I'll tell you whose it is."

turns.player: Use Order-Sense on the traveling case at Fendt's feet — it contains the real filtration readings, but the case itself may be part of a larger design; read what it belongs to.
turns.summary: Order-Sense reads the traveling case as the knot in a deliberate disclosure: Fendt has been staging this handoff, not fleeing it.
turns.narration:
You drop your gaze to the traveling case without announcement — just a quiet shift of attention, the kind Fendt won't register as anything but thought. Order-Sense opens in you the way a door opens when you already know the room: not revelation, recognition.

The case is real. The readings inside it are real. But the case isn't the end of the thread — it's a knot in a longer cord. The pattern you're looking at runs: Fendt's eleven years of accumulated data, the preliminary concern notice that named the seventh band, the formal ledger rewritten afterward, the calibration files removed before anyone thought to check them, and beneath all of it, older than any of those moves, the array specification itself. The case and its paper exist inside that structure the way a splinter exists inside a wound — not the cause, but the evidence that something went in.

The lattice here is thin and the sense runs lean because of it, but what it returns is enough: this case belongs to a disclosure. A deliberate one, staged and timed. Fendt didn't pack it because he was fleeing — he packed it because he was waiting for someone to show up who could carry it somewhere the structure couldn't reach. The design the case is part of isn't Grael's design. It's Fendt's. He built himself into a position where the real paper only matters if it gets out of this building in someone else's hands.

You let the sense close. Two energy, clean. You don't look at the case again.

turns.player: Bring Pell's offer to a head — ask Fendt to reproduce the margin notation now. A glyph, a numeral, whatever he remembers. If her father's hand matches, the authorship chain closes in this room.
turns.summary: Pell confirms the margin notation as her father's hand — the authorship chain closes in the upper room.

turns.player: Ask Pell directly whether she knew — or half-knew — what her father's work was. She named the hand without hesitating. That's not memory; that's a thing kept ready.
turns.summary: Pell admits she half-knew — the crate of instruments, the trained-out hand, a father who called it over and said no more.
turns.narration:
You let the notebook sit between the three of you for a moment — Fendt still on the desk edge, Huginn motionless on your shoulder — and then you look at Pell directly. Not an accusation. A question that's been sitting in the room since she named the hand without pausing.

"Did you know?" you ask. Quiet, no edge to it. "Or half-know. You said *surveyor's habit* like it was already in your mouth."

She doesn't look away. That's the first thing. She holds your eye for the space of two slow breaths, and something passes through her expression that isn't quite surprise and isn't quite relief — the look of someone who has been waiting to be asked the right question and wasn't certain it would come. "Half," she says, finally. "There was a crate in the workshop — tools I wasn't to touch. Old marking instruments, a set of graduated lenses he said weren't his trade anymore. He never explained and I never pushed." Her jaw sets slightly. "The hand I knew because he taught me ledger-work. Every three he wrote, I wrote the same way, until I trained it out. It felt too much like him."

Fendt has gone very still on the desk edge. Not uncomfortable — the stillness of a man recalibrating weight. He looks at Pell with the careful attention of someone adjusting a figure he had written in as fixed. "He didn't tell you," Fendt says. Not a question. An accounting.

"He told me it was over," Pell says. "That it was someone else's problem now, and the valley was better for the distance." She sets one finger on the notebook page without touching the notation itself. "He was not a man who lied. But he was a man who could make a sentence do the work of ten."

turns.player: Use The Attended End on Fendt — read what is ending for him right now, how far along it is, and whether any path turns it.
turns.summary: The Attended End reads Fendt's ending: six weeks, one path, and Silas names it plainly.
turns.narration:
You don't announce it. The Attended End opens the way it always does — a settling, a quiet behind the eyes, the world narrowing to what is actually in front of you.

Fendt's ending is close. Not today, not this week — but it has shape now, the kind a thing gets when its conditions have been satisfied. You read it the way you'd read a weight-bearing beam that's been cut halfway through: still standing, still holding, but the calculation has already been made. The marking on the board was the cut. The missing ledger record was the second. Whatever appeal he might have filed, the channel for it was closed before he knew it existed. The ending has a name — exile, stripped post, the slow social death of someone who can no longer be vouched for — and it has a direction: six weeks, maybe eight, before the administrative machinery grinds to a conclusion the council doesn't even have to endorse formally. Grael doesn't need to move again. He only has to wait.

Whether any path turns it: one. The paper in Fendt's coat, carried by someone outside the Radiant hierarchy, to a record that can be formally lodged before the review closes. That window is not long. You can feel the specific shape of it — a narrow thing, already narrowing.

You hold it for a moment without speaking. Huginn shifts his weight on your shoulder, very slightly, and is still again. You look at Fendt — the coat, the slept-in press of it, the case at his boots — and you say, level and without softening it: "Six weeks before it closes. One path. The paper needs to be lodged formally, outside this hierarchy, before the review lands. That's what you staged this for. So where does it go?"

turns.player: Pell... I want to know what you want to do here.  Do we look for your father? Do we try to save Fendt?  I am ready to go as deep and far as you want.
turns.summary: Pell names her priority — Fendt first, the paper lodged before the six-week window closes, her father held in reserve.
turns.narration:
The room holds the three of you in morning light — Fendt still on the desk edge with his own timeline folded into his coat, Pell with one finger still resting near the notebook page, Huginn weight-settled on your shoulder and saying nothing. You turn to Pell.

"Pell." Just her name first, giving her the room. Then: "I want to know what you want to do here. Do we look for your father? Do we try to save Fendt? I'm ready to go as deep and far as you want."

She doesn't answer immediately. Her finger lifts from the notebook. She looks at the copied notation — her father's three-hand, the loop closing before the downstroke, leaning left, unmistakably his — and then she looks at Fendt. A real look, not a read. The kind that takes the measure of a person rather than a problem.

"Fendt's solvable," she says finally. Her voice is level, but there's something working underneath it — the controlled register she uses when something is costing her and she'd rather you not watch the cost. "The paper goes to formal lodgment outside the Radiant hierarchy. That's the path you named. It's the right one." A beat. "My father's not solvable the same way. He left. He made a choice about what he'd carry and what he'd put down. I don't know that he wants finding." She straightens, and something settles in her — not resolution exactly, more like a decision to hold two things at once without letting either one collapse the other. "But Fendt has six weeks. That's the clock we're working to. We lodge the paper, we name Grael for what he did, and we follow whatever thread that opens." Her eyes come up to yours. Direct, honest, and carrying more than she's saying. "If that thread leads to my father, I'll decide then what I want from him. I'm not looking for him. But I'm not turning away if the work brings us there."

turns.player: Fendt - we will carry this burden.  We will save the valley.  We need your cooperation and assistance.  Write down everything - we will protect you if needed, but it won't come to that. Go  to Millbrook and tell Mara Wells we sent you - give her this (give him a piece of dried starfruit) and she'll know what to do.  We will bring justice and get to the bottom of this.  We will find the engineer who can help us stop what is coming.
turns.summary: Fendt writes down everything, accepts the starfruit token, and agrees to walk to Millbrook and find Mara Wells. Silas and Pell depart the Edge District and reach Millbrook by evening.
turns.narration:
You pull the sealed packet of dried starfruit from your pack — old pass circuit goods, three or four winters old, still good — and set it on the desk between you and Fendt. Not a ceremony. A key.

"Fendt." You keep your voice level, not a rallying speech, just a man naming what is real. "We'll carry this. Write down everything you know — the readings, the dates, the gap between when you filed your concern notice and when Grael rewrote the ledger. All of it. Keep nothing in your head that isn't also on paper somewhere we can reach it. Then go to Millbrook. Find a woman named Mara Wells — she keeps the supply store. Give her the starfruit and tell her we sent you. She'll know what to do with you, and she will not ask you questions you don't want answering in public." You let that land for a moment. "You won't need protecting. Grael's method works because the machinery is quiet. We're going to make it loud."

Fendt looks at the dried starfruit for two breaths. Then he picks it up — gently, which tells you more than his face does — and tucks it into his coat pocket beside the real readings. He opens his notebook to a clean page and writes for three minutes without pausing: dates, band designations, the nine-day gap between the fifth and sixth falsified entries, Grael's name, the preliminary notice he filed and the date it was received. He tears the page out with care, folds it once, and holds it out to you. "One copy with you. One stays in the lining of the case." He nods at the traveling case at his boots. "If something goes wrong before I reach Millbrook, the case goes with whoever finds me."

Pell says nothing. She takes the folded page from Fendt and hands it to you — her fingers steady, her expression somewhere between resolved and the kind of still that comes after a decision you can't undo. Huginn shifts once on your shoulder, resettles, and is quiet. You leave the building the way you came, through the rear service lane, past the cloth-wrapped bundle still leaning against the iron-banded door, into the early light of the Edge District.

The road back to Millbrook takes most of the day — the pass descent, the valley floor opening beneath you in long afternoon gold, the north road into familiar smoke and the smell of the river running gray-green at its edges. You arrive as the cookhouse fires are being banked for the evening, Memory on your shoulder, Pell one step behind, the real readings folded into your coat and Fendt somewhere behind you on the road with a piece of starfruit and a direction.

*You feel it settle into you — a new steadiness. (Level 10: +1 attunement, +5 reserves, and growth to choose — a sub-attribute point and a skill point.)*

turns.player: Gather any useful supplies or tools from Fendt's shop before leaving to make our way to find the engineer.
turns.summary: Silas scavenges Fendt's shop for useful supplies, then a passing traveler warns of gray seepage at the lower ford.
turns.narration:
Fendt's shop is small and carefully kept — the kind of order a man maintains when the rest of his situation is unraveling. While Pell checks the road and Huginn settles on the window ledge, you move through the back room with a practical eye: shelves of graduated vessels, a rack of glass rods, labeled jars of reagent powder. Most of it is Edge District filtration work, too specialized to carry. But the useful things present themselves readily, the way they do in a well-run space.

A coil of fine brass wire on a hook by the door — the kind used to seal sample vessels, precise enough for binding work. A small wax-sealed tin of chalk dust, brilliant white, for marking surfaces in dark conditions. And on the lowest shelf, half-hidden behind a set of measuring cylinders, a folded square of filtering cloth: tight-woven, pre-Transition make by the feel of it, the kind that could strain contaminated water through three times and still hold. You tuck all three into the Traveler's Pack without ceremony.

Pell appears at the doorway as you straighten. She reads the pack's new weight in one glance and gives a small nod — she'd have looked in the same places. Huginn steps off the window ledge and returns to your shoulder, and the three of you walk out into the afternoon light. The road south through the valley will take what remains of the day. Fendt's door swings shut behind you.

Half a mile out, where the pine run opens onto the old trade track, you meet a woman coming the other way — a thin, road-worn figure with a pack nearly as loaded as yours, her coat tied at the waist with orange cord. She stops when she sees you and holds up one open hand: a traveler's greeting, nothing asked for. "The ford at the lower crossing is passable," she says, without preamble, "but the stones on the east side have been seeping something gray. Don't fill your skins there." She looks at Huginn once, unbothered, then steps aside to let you pass. No name offered. No price. The valley is not only teeth.

turns.player: Use The Attended End on the woman — she stopped without being asked and offered something freely; read what is ending for her
turns.summary: The Attended End reads the traveler's ending — a road completing, not a life. Silas nods his respect and the party prepares to move.
turns.narration:
You let the ability settle quietly — no ceremony, just attention opening the way it does when Marrow wills it. The traveler is standing in afternoon light with her hand still open, pack heavy on one shoulder, coat tied with orange cord. She's not nervous. She's not waiting for anything.

What The Attended End finds is small and precise: a road ending. Not her life — her circuit. She has walked this track longer than most people walk any one thing, and she is walking it for the last time. Not from grief or failure. From completion. The thing she carried — the pattern of warnings, the checking of crossings, the habit of stopping when something needed saying — is nearly done being hers. Someone else will carry it next season, or no one will, and she has made her peace with either.

There is nothing to turn aside. This is not a death and it is not a crisis. It is the ordinary closing of a chapter, and she knows it in her bones without having named it yet.

Huginn makes no sound. That is confirmation enough. You look at the traveler — her open hand, the weight of the pack, the way she is standing like someone who has already said the important thing — and you give her a nod of genuine respect. "Good road," you say, meaning it as specifically as you can.

turns.player: Let her go with only the nod — keep moving south toward Leth and the engineer before the afternoon runs out.
turns.summary: The road traveler departs southbound; Silas, Pell, and Huginn press south toward the Edge District and Leth.
turns.narration:
You hold the nod a beat longer than courtesy requires — enough that she knows it was received, not just returned. Then you shift Memory's lash at your shoulder and step back onto the track.

She doesn't watch you go. By the time Pell falls into step beside you and Huginn lifts from the fence post at the road's edge, the traveler is already moving again, southbound, the orange cord at her coat swinging with the rhythm of a walk that knows where it's going. You don't look back either. The afternoon is still long enough, barely, to reach the Edge District before the light goes.

The pine run thins out over the next quarter mile, the canopy opening onto open road, last season's grass flat and straw-colored at the verges. The trade track here is well-worn — two ruts and a center strip of compressed earth, the kind of road that accumulates use without anyone deciding to build it. Pell walks on your left, pack settled, saying nothing yet. Huginn rides your right shoulder, facing forward.

"Leth first," Pell says, not a question. She's been running the sequence since you left Fendt's shop. "If she has his name and you have the shape of what he'd need to hear, that's a shorter conversation than wandering the district asking after a difficult man who doesn't want to be found." She glances at Huginn. "Unless the bird has a preference." Huginn does not dignify this. Pell almost smiles.

turns.player: Head straight to Leth — find the archivist and get the engineer's name before she closes up for the day
turns.summary: Silas and Pell find Leth in her archive room at the Edge District — she has Sorel's letter and is ready to talk.
turns.narration:
The Edge District resolves out of the afternoon haze as the pine run gives way entirely — a compact settlement pressed against the cliff face, buildings of pale stone and salvaged timber running in two crooked rows along a central lane. The kind of place that has been a stopping point for so long it forgot to become a destination. Lanterns are just beginning to glow in a few windows, though the sun is still above the ridge.

Pell reads the scrap from her coat pocket without breaking stride. "Archivist type. Kept careful records." She folds it back. "That means a building with more organization than the rest of them."

Huginn lifts from your shoulder and angles left without being asked — toward a low stone building set slightly back from the lane, its door propped open with a round river rock, a small board above it scratched with what might once have been a proper sign. The bird lands on the board's edge and settles, watching the entrance.

Inside, the smell is old paper and lamp-smoke. The room is lined floor to ceiling with wooden shelving — not chaotic, not precious, just relentlessly organized, every bundle and ledger tied and labeled in a small tight hand. A woman in her mid-fifties stands at a slanted desk near the window, writing without looking up. She finishes the line before she speaks. "The bird on my sign is yours, I assume." She sets the pen in its groove. "Sorel sent a letter. It arrived this morning, which means you left the day before yesterday." She studies you both with the flat professional attention of someone whose job is to notice what things actually are. "You're looking for Grael's engineer. Sit down — I have ground to cover before I hand you a name."

turns.player: Sit and listen — let Leth cover her ground at her own pace before asking anything
turns.summary: Silas settles and listens as Leth opens the archive breach — she's covering her ground before giving the name.
turns.narration:
The round river rock holds the door propped at its usual angle, and the lamp on Leth's slanted desk throws a steady yellow pool across the nearest shelf-row. You pull the low stool from beside the door and settle onto it without ceremony — Memory's shaft resting against the wall, Fendt's folded testimony quiet in your coat. Pell takes the wall beside the door frame, arms loose, watching Leth the way she watches anything she hasn't assessed yet: not hostile, not open, just present.

Leth doesn't acknowledge the sitting. She sets Sorel's letter precisely at the upper-right corner of the desk — filed, not discarded — and picks up her pen, then sets it down again. A woman who organizes herself before she speaks. "Sorel's letter says you're looking for someone who can read buried pre-Transition hydraulic work." She says it as a statement, not a question. "It also says you're not from the Radiant hierarchy, which is the useful part." She moves to the second shelf from the left — her hand goes directly to a bundle, no searching — and pulls a thin document roll, sets it on the desk but doesn't open it. "There are two things I want you to understand before I give you a name. The first is about the archive breach. The second is about what the breach means for whoever you're going to find — because those two things are not separate, and I won't have you walking in blind."

Huginn shifts on the sign board outside. A single, deliberate readjustment — settling weight onto one foot, then the other. Leth hears it. Her eyes move briefly to the open door, then back to you, recalibrating something internal. "The breach was six weeks ago." She smooths the document roll with one hand, still closed. "Calibration files. Pre-Transition provenance. The person who took them knew the room — not the public layout, the working layout. The secondary stack, third tier, east corner. That is not a thing you know from asking at the desk."

She pauses, and you let the pause sit. "Good," she says, though you didn't say anything. She means the listening. Her hand moves to the document roll but doesn't open it — whatever the second thing is, she's deciding how to begin it.

turns.player: Watch Huginn rather than Leth for a moment — the bird adjusted on a word she spoke; read what it's attending to
turns.summary: Huginn's attention names the document roll as the thing worth reading — the bird is attending to it, not Leth.
turns.narration:
Huginn hasn't moved from the sign board — the grey-black shape of it pressed flat against the weathered wood, rain-soaked at the edges, but its eye has shifted. Not to Leth. Not to you. It's fixed on the unopened document roll on the desk.

You've traveled long enough with the bird to know the difference between its patience and its attention. Patience is the stillness it holds at a fire or a long road camp — settled, unhurried, present the way stone is present. This is the other thing. The eye is the color of old iron and it does not blink, and it is reading the roll the way it reads a thing that is ending.

Not Leth. Not the shelves. The roll.

You look at it again: thin, tied off with a strip of dark waxed cord, the paper denser than the ledger bundles on the walls. Archive stock — preservation weight. Whatever is in it wasn't filed with the others. It's on the desk because Leth brought it here deliberately, today, and hasn't shown it yet.

Leth is still speaking, steady and sequenced, covering her ground at her own pace. She hasn't looked at the roll since she set it down. That, you notice, is also a choice. Pell's eyes cut briefly to you — she caught the stillness in Huginn too, though she doesn't say anything, only adjusts her weight against the door frame and settles back to watching.

turns.player: Let Leth finish her sequence without interruption — she'll open the roll when she's ready; don't break her ground
turns.summary: Leth names the engineer — Calvar, above the cooperage on the east lane — and opens the document roll: a diagram in his own hand, left with her three days ago.
turns.narration:
Leth picks up her pen again, turns it once, sets it down. The lamp holds steady. Outside, the Edge District has gone to its evening rhythm — a cart wheel somewhere, a door, then quiet. You keep your eyes on the shelf-row behind her head rather than the roll, giving her the room to move at her own pace.

Pell shifts her weight against the door frame, barely. Huginn doesn't blink.

"The meaning for the engineer," Leth says, as though resuming a sentence she'd simply paused mid-word, "is that whoever took those files knows what he built. Not what the array does — what he designed, specifically. The seventh-band monitoring specification. The calibration tolerances he set for the lower filtration reach." She taps the desk once with her fingernail, not for emphasis — for precision. "If that material is in someone else's hands, the engineer's value as a witness changes. He can still name what he saw. He cannot prove the design was his, because the documents that would prove it are gone."

She crosses to the desk and unties the waxed cord on the roll in three practiced movements. Huginn's eye follows her hands. The paper opens flat — not a letter, not a ledger entry. A diagram. Two columns of notation in a cramped, careful hand, and at the top a name written in a hand you know: the same distinctive three-formation Pell identified in Fendt's copied margin notes. Below it, a single line in different ink — fresher, written more recently. Leth doesn't hand it to you. She turns it so you can read it from where you sit.

"His name is Calvar," she says. "He has a room above the cooperage on the east lane. He has not left the Edge District since the Transition, which means he has been here for fifteen years watching the array run on specifications he wrote and told no one about." A pause. "He will not want to go to Millbrook. He will not want to go anywhere. But he came to me three days ago and left this." She touches the edge of the diagram without covering the notation. "Which means some part of him already knows he has to."

Huginn lets out a single sound from the sign board — low, dry, a syllable that is almost a word. Leth glances at it without expression. "Your bird has opinions," she says.

turns.player: Study the diagram itself — use Order-Sense to read what design it is part of and what it is not showing
turns.summary: Order-Sense reads the diagram as a proof-of-authorship anchor, not a full map — and reveals a second hand in fresher ink at the lower-right corner.
turns.narration:
You step to the desk and let Order-Sense open — two energy drawn thin through the starved lattice, the field pressing back like a hand through wet canvas. The diagram steadies under your attention anyway.

Calvar's notation is clean and practiced: two columns of band tolerances, instrument callouts, a set of reference marks at the lower margin. What the diagram shows is the seventh-band monitoring array as he left it — calibration points, cross-reference intervals, the spatial relationship of the lower filtration ledge to the intake manifold. Clear, precise, complete on its own terms.

Order-Sense does not read documents. It reads designs. And what it shows you is the shape this diagram is a fragment of — the larger thing it was cut from. The diagram is not a record of the full array. It is specifically the seventh-band section. The rest of the design is present in its absence: the upper bands, the primary intake, the northeastern branching conduit that the seventh band was built to monitor. Not described. Not drawn. Deliberately excluded. Calvar did not bring Leth a full accounting of what he built. He brought her the piece that proves he built it — the piece that proves his authorship of the tolerance specifications now in someone else's hands. A statement of origin, not a map. An anchor.

And in the lower-right corner, partially obscured by the dark waxed cord when it was tied: a second line in fresher ink, a different hand entirely. Not Calvar's three-formation. Smaller, flatter, written in haste — four characters and a symbol you don't recognize, but the hand is controlled even at speed. Someone else has been here since Calvar left this. Someone read it and marked it.

turns.player: Say nothing to Leth yet — use Palework on the fresher ink itself: read how recently it was written and what state the hand was in
turns.summary: Palework reads the fresher ink as added within forty-eight hours — fast, deliberate hand, a symbol written with unusual care, added after Calvar left the diagram with Leth.
turns.narration:
You rest two fingers at the lower-right corner of the diagram without quite touching the ink — close enough that Palework can find its edge. Three energy draws down, but you hold it light, barely a pressure. Reading the already-finished is what this craft does best, and the ink is finished enough.

What comes back is recent. The ink dried within the last forty-eight hours — no more. And it came from a hand that was working fast. Not panicked, not trembling, but moving with the compressed economy of someone who has very little time and knows it. The four characters were pressed in with the kind of deliberate force you use when you need the mark to stay, whatever else happens. The unrecognized symbol beside them is different — it went on last, and the hand slowed for it, deliberate in a way the characters were not. Whatever that symbol means to whoever wrote it, they wanted to get it right.

Leth is watching you. She has not moved and has not asked. Pell's gaze drops from your face to the corner of the diagram and back up — she's read that you read something.

Calvar left this diagram three days ago. Whatever is in that lower-right corner was added after he was gone.

turns.player: Ask Leth directly — has anyone else handled or viewed this diagram since Calvar left it?
turns.summary: Leth confirms no one was given access to the diagram — someone entered her room during the night and added the symbol without her knowledge.
turns.narration:
You lift your fingers from the corner of the diagram and look up at Leth. The lamp between you holds the room in amber — her face, the shelves behind her, the open roll.

"Has anyone else handled this since Calvar left it? Seen it, even?"

Leth's answer is immediate, and that itself is telling. "No. He brought it himself — morning, three days ago, before the first light-guide shift. Set it on the desk, told me to keep it off the shelves and under the cord until someone came asking about the seventh band or the lower filtration ledge. Those were his words, exactly." A pause. "I filed Sorel's letter this morning. No one came before you."

She looks at the diagram, then at you, then at your fingers hovering near the lower-right corner. "Something's been added." It isn't a question. She has noticed the way your hand was positioned — the archivist's eye has been tracking you since you touched it. "I did not add it. The roll has been under the cord on this desk, in this room, with the door propped — but I sleep in the next room, and I am not awake every hour." Her voice carries no defensiveness, only the flat precision of a woman filing an accurate report. "Someone came in during the night. One of the two nights since he left it."

Pell straightens in the door frame. Outside, Huginn shifts on the sign-board — one small repositioning, weight to the other foot, the way the bird moves when something has just been confirmed rather than discovered. The Edge District evening presses at the open door: the faint braid of light rising from the street guides below, the smell of lamp oil and cooling stone.

turns.player: Use Order-Sense on the room itself — read what design the archive room is part of and how the entry was made
turns.summary: Order-Sense traces the archive room's design and locates the shared rear corridor — the entry route used to add the symbol.
turns.narration:
You turn from the diagram and let Order-Sense widen — a quiet cast, two energy drawn through the thin lattice with the deliberate economy of someone who knows the substrate is running at half-depth. No surge; just attention opened a little further than ordinary looking.

The archive room resolves. Low stone building, yes — but Order-Sense shows you the design it belongs to: a chain of three such rooms sharing a roof-line and a common rear access corridor. Leth's is the middle one. The door to that corridor is not the propped-front door — it is a second door, flush with the shelving on the east wall, visible now because Order-Sense shows you the seam where it sits. The latch side is on the right. It has been used recently: the dust on the floor in front of it is disturbed in a short arc, the kind a door's base sweeps through when it opens inward.

That is how the entry was made. Not through the front, where Leth would have heard it. Through the shared corridor that connects all three rooms — a route any tenant in the row would know, and any insider.

turns.player: Ask Leth who else occupies the other rooms in this row — and whether any of them would have known Calvar was leaving something
turns.summary: Leth names the other two occupants — an empty survey office to the west, and Vash the lens-grinder to the east, who was working the morning Calvar came.
turns.narration:
You turn toward the east-wall seam — the door still flush with the shelving, dust arc undisturbed since you named it — and nod toward it before looking back at Leth.

"The other rooms in this row. Who occupies them?"
